ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists for yellow dal tadka as a composite dish. FOODON_03311737 (lentil food product) is the closest parent covering the lentil component. The steamed rice component is not captured by this code.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (Lentils, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor for the dal component. No composite FoodEx2 code exists for dal tadka with steamed rice as a restaurant dish.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept found for yellow dal tadka with steamed rice as a composite dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant serving of yellow dal tadka with steamed rice is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 200 g of cooked dal and 200 g of steamed white rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Toor Dal (cooked) and Cooked Rice (plain, milled)
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values are derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017 edition) for cooked toor dal and plain cooked white rice. The composite dish (dal + rice, approximately 50:50 by weight) is estimated by weighted average of the two components. Ghee contribution to fat and saturated fat is incorporated based on a standard tadka quantity of approximately 5 g ghee per 100 g dal. Sodium is estimated from typical restaurant preparation including salt added during cooking.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Lentils (Lens culinaris) and White rice (boiled)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.source_match_notes: The composite GI is estimated as a weighted average of cooked lentils (GI ~29, University of Sydney GI Database) and boiled white rice (GI ~72, University of Sydney GI Database), weighted 50:50 by mass, yielding a blended GI of approximately 41. This is consistent with published evidence that lentil-rice combinations substantially lower the overall GI compared to rice alone, due to the high fibre and protein content of lentils attenuating glycaemic response. GL per serving calculated as: (GI × carbohydrate_g_per_serving) / 100 = (41 × 82) / 100 = 33.6, where carbohydrate per 400 g serving = 82 g.

hypertension.guidance: A standard 400 g restaurant serving provides approximately 840 mg sodium, representing 36–42% of the recommended daily limit of 2000–2300 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate glycaemic load from white rice component
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated composite GI of 41 (low) is favourable due to the lentil component, but the glycaemic load per full serving is approximately 33.6 g, which is high; individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion of rice or substitute with brown rice to reduce postprandial glucose excursion.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Beneficial lentil fibre and protein with modest saturated fat from ghee
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Yellow lentils provide soluble fibre and plant protein that support LDL cholesterol reduction; the ghee-based tadka contributes approximately 3.6 g saturated fat per serving, which is within acceptable limits for most cardiovascular disease management plans when consumed in moderation.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Yellow Dal Tadka with Steamed Rice is a classic North Indian comfort meal consisting of two components: a spiced yellow lentil curry (dal tadka) and plain steamed white rice. The dal is prepared from toor dal (split pigeon peas, Cajanus cajan) or moong dal (split mung beans), which are pressure-cooked or simmered until soft and then finished with a tadka — a tempering of ghee, cumin seeds, mustard seeds, dried red chillies, garlic, and sometimes onion and tomato — poured over the cooked lentils to infuse aromatic flavour. The dish has a smooth, slightly thick consistency with a warm, earthy, mildly spiced flavour profile and a rich aroma from the ghee tempering. Steamed white rice is served alongside or beneath the dal, providing a neutral starchy base that balances the savoury, slightly tangy lentil curry. Nutritionally, the dish is a good source of plant protein (approximately 4.2 g per 100 g), dietary fibre (1.6 g per 100 g), and complex carbohydrates (20.5 g per 100 g). The lentil component contributes folate, iron, potassium, and B vitamins. The composite glycaemic index is estimated at 41 (low), making it more glycaemically favourable than plain rice alone, though the glycaemic load per full restaurant serving remains moderate to high due to portion size. Clinically, the dish is suitable for most dietary patterns but warrants sodium awareness in hypertensive individuals and portion control for those managing Type 2 Diabetes. It is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal, making it broadly accessible across dietary requirements. In Singapore, this dish is commonly served at Indian restaurants and hawker stalls as a main meal.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values are estimated from ICMR-NIN reference data for cooked toor dal and plain cooked white rice combined in a 50:50 weighted average, with ghee contribution incorporated for the tadka. No exact composite dish entry for yellow dal tadka with steamed rice exists in ICMR-NIN or HPB Singapore Foodo. GI is an analogous estimate based on University of Sydney GI Database values for lentils and white rice. Confidence is therefore medium rather than high.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are a weighted composite estimate (50% cooked toor dal, 50% steamed white rice by mass) derived from ICMR-NIN Nutritive Value of Indian Foods (2017). A standard tadka of approximately 5 g ghee per 100 g dal has been incorporated into fat and saturated fat values. 2. Sodium is estimated at 210 mg per 100 g based on typical restaurant preparation; actual values may vary significantly by restaurant. 3. vitamin_b12_mcg is 0.0 as this is a plant-based dish containing no animal-derived ingredients except ghee, which contains negligible B12; confirmed zero for practical purposes. 4. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this food combination and is not expected to be a significant source. 5. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this food combination. 6. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in ICMR-NIN for this food combination. 7. trans_fat_g is 0.0 as ghee contains negligible industrially produced trans fats; naturally occurring ruminant trans fats are present in trace amounts below reporting threshold — confirmed zero for practical purposes. 8. GI of 41 is a weighted analogous estimate (lentils GI 29, white rice GI 72, 50:50 blend); this is consistent with published literature on lentil-rice combinations. GL per 400 g serving = (41 × 82) / 100 = 33.6 g. 9. FoodOn and FoodEx2 codes are closest-parent matches; no exact composite dish code exists for this preparation. 10. Restaurant source (Manam - Upper Circular Road, field survey data) is preserved as provenance context only and is not used as a nutritional evidence source.

improvement_actions_taken: Constructed composite nutrient profile from ICMR-NIN cooked toor dal and cooked white rice data (50:50 weighted average) with ghee tadka contribution.
Estimated GI as weighted average of lentils (GI 29) and white rice (GI 72) from University of Sydney GI Database.
Added mandatory multilingual names for Indian dish served in Singapore: Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, Malay, Tamil, Hindi, romanized.
Populated aliases_by_language with all language groups and verified consistency with flat aliases list.
Documented all -1 sentinel values (vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, selenium_mcg) with explicit reasons in notes.
Documented 0.0 values (trans_fat_g, vitamin_b12_mcg) with confirmed zero justification in notes.
Set gi_category to 'low' correctly for GI = 41 (≤ 55 threshold).
Used only closest_parent and none for ontology match types; no invented codes.
Included specific clinical guidance for all three health conditions.
